title: The Wells Go Quiet
body: The aquifer crisis arrives not as a flood or a fire but as a stillness: across the doabs, the tubewells begin to pull air. The famine generation is born into rationed water and inherited debt. In the gurdwaras, the langar does not close — it is the one institution that does not close — and people begin to notice that fact and think about it.

title: Panj Paani Ahd — The Treaty of Five Rivers
body: Negotiated in a tent on a dry riverbed by hydrologists, farm unions, and two governments exhausted past pride, the Ahd does something no water treaty had done: it makes the rivers themselves the beneficiaries, with standing in both nations' courts. The phrase 'the water of the unborn' enters legal language. Cynics call it poetry. The poets, correctly, take this as a compliment.

title: The First Langar Grid
body: Three hundred gurdwara kitchens, mosque langars, and temple bhandaras federate their solar roofs, grain stores, and kitchens into a single mutual-aid mesh. It is built to survive famine; it accidentally becomes the region's most reliable power and food utility. The design principle, lifted directly from the langar floor: everyone sits at the same level, everyone eats, no one is asked why they are hungry.

title: The Vaisakhi of a Million Saplings
body: The Great Replanting begins on a single Vaisakhi morning, timed to the hour across the watershed. Diaspora families — the Returned — arrive through the whole decade that follows, carrying seed varieties preserved in gardens on five continents. Doaba's graft libraries are founded to receive them. The slogan painted on the first library wall: 'Panjab kept itself safe in the pockets of the scattered.'

title: The Border Bloom
body: Demilitarisation of the fence-line begins as a soil remediation project and gets away from everyone involved, in the best way. Villages on both sides, asked to maintain 'their' kilometre, begin maintaining it together because that is simply easier. By the time the two parliaments formally consecrate Sanjha Bagh, the garden has existed in practice for years. The treaty mostly catches the law up to the beekeepers.

title: Founding of the Sarovar Crescent
body: Majha commits to the pool lattice: ten thousand sarovars in concentric rings, dug over twenty years, banking monsoon against summer. The engineering is borrowed from the oldest pattern in the region — the sacred pool beside the place of gathering — and scaled until the metaphor becomes the municipality.

title: The Salt Archive Opens
body: Pothohar rededicates the salt-range mines as the watershed's memory: seed duplicates, testimony recordings of the famine generation, every treaty, every rainfall record. The founding archivists set the admission rule still in force — the Archive is free to enter and forbidden to summarise. You must read the actual years.

title: The Sunfield Covenant
body: Malwa retires the last of the debt-cotton acreage and raises the dhup-seva canopies — solar commons held by panchayat trusts, shade shared with grazing and lentils beneath. The covenant is signed by every village that lost someone to the debt years, and the list of names is etched into the first canopy pylon, facing sunrise.

title: The Children's Renewal
body: The Glacier Compact comes up for its fifty-year renewal, and the Trust does something unprecedented: it rules that only signatories under sixteen may sign, since they alone will live under its full term. Four thousand children climb to the headwaters that summer. The renewal ceremony — a cupped hand of meltwater, drunk facing downstream — becomes the pilgrimage every watershed child now makes.

title: The Quiet Century Declared
body: A generation that has never known the wells to fail votes, region by region, to adopt the Quiet Century: a hundred-year moratorium on extraction beyond replenishment, anywhere in the watershed. It passes everywhere, widest in the regions that remember worst. The celebration is deliberately small. The point, the organisers say, is that there should be nothing to celebrate — only a promise kept at the ordinary scale, daily, by everyone.

title: The Present Hour
body: This is where the chronicle stands: amrit vela of the world's long day. The rivers run clear and argued-over, the langar grid hums, the Bagh needs weeding, the ice is holding. Nothing is finished. In Chardi Kala nothing is ever finished — the rising is the point. The chroniclers leave this entry open on purpose.
